summary: Reis argues that the biggest gap in the data industry isn't tooling—it's the lack of foundational skills and knowledge to properly leverage those tools. He advocates spending 60-70% of learning time on fundamentals (classic books, papers, underlying principles) because they provide stable foundations for evaluating the constantly changing landscape of technologies and tools.
key_points: The skill and knowledge gap in data is massive, not a tooling problem
Foundational knowledge (principles, classic books) should take 60-70% of learning time
Applied skills translate foundations to specific tools your team uses
Without fundamentals, you'll either duct-tape problems or cause catastrophic failures
Problems from decades ago are the same ones we face today
money_quotes: The most significant gap in our industry isn't tooling... we need to level up our skills and knowledge to leverage the incredible power at our fingertips properly.
To paraphrase Charlie Munger, without foundational understanding, 'you are like a one-legged man in an ass-kicking contest.'
Skip the step of understanding how servers, networking, storage, and distributed computing work, and you're dead in the water.
At a minimum, you'll duct tape over problems, incurring all forms of debt at high interest rates that'll need to be paid back.
It's not tooling that holds us back. The big challenge is the mastery, knowledge, and understanding of how to use these tools from first principles.
key_insight: Mastering foundational knowledge—not chasing new tools—is what separates competent data professionals from those who accumulate technical debt and eventually fail catastrophically.
